model_narrative: We ship a single commodity from a set of supply plants to a set of demand markets over a fixed set of allowable routes. For each route we decide how many cases to ship. The cost of a route grows with the square root of the volume shipped on it, a concave shipping economy that is approximated by a piecewise-linear curve built from continuous per-segment fill amounts and binary per-segment activation choices. The objective is to minimize the total transportation cost across all routes.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the supply plants that ship goods

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the demand markets that receive goods

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the segments of the piecewise-linear curve that approximates the square-root cost, listed from the lowest volume range to the highest; the first segment s0 is the unbounded anchor segment below the first breakpoint and the last segment s6 is the unbounded tail segment above the last breakpoint

sets.name: sl
sets.doc: the four data fields stored for each piecewise-linear segment: x is the segment's starting volume breakpoint, y is the square-root curve value at that breakpoint, l is the horizontal length (width) of the segment, and g is the slope of the curve over the segment

sets.name: ij
sets.doc: the set of valid plant-to-market shipping routes, one ordered pair per allowable route; here every plant can ship to every market so it is the full product of plants and markets

params.name: a
params.doc: the supply capacity available at each plant, in cases; total shipments out of a plant may not exceed this

params.name: b
params.doc: the demand requirement at each market, in cases; total shipments into a market must meet at least this

params.name: d
params.doc: the distance from a plant to a market, in thousands of miles

params.name: f
params.doc: the freight rate, in dollars per case per thousand miles; a single scalar

params.name: c
params.doc: the per-case transport cost on a route, in thousands of dollars per case, computed as the freight rate times the route distance divided by one thousand

params.name: sqrtp
params.doc: the piecewise-linear curve data table indexed by segment and field; sqrtp[s,'x'] is the starting volume breakpoint of segment s, sqrtp[s,'y'] is the curve height at that breakpoint, sqrtp[s,'l'] is the width of segment s, and sqrtp[s,'g'] is the slope over segment s. The anchor segment s0 carries a sentinel width of -1 and the tail segment s6 a sentinel width of 1; only segments with a width strictly between 0 and 1000 are genuine bounded segments

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity shipped on each route, in cases

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all routes, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: delta
vars.doc: the amount of a route's shipped volume that falls within a given segment of the piecewise-linear curve, in cases; the fill of segment s for that route measured from the segment's starting breakpoint

vars.name: y_seg
vars.doc: the segment-activation indicator for a route; equals 1 if that segment of the piecewise-linear curve is the active one for the route and 0 otherwise

vars.name: sqrtx
vars.doc: the piecewise-linear approximation of the square root of the route's shipped volume

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each plant can ship out only as much as it has available. For every plant, the total shipped to all markets it serves must not exceed that plant's supply capacity.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every market's requirement must be covered. For each market, the total received from all plants that serve it must be at least that market's demand.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: The volume shipped on a route is reconstructed from the piecewise-linear segment encoding. For each route, the shipped volume equals the sum over all segments of the segment's starting breakpoint counted when that segment is the active one, plus the fill amount placed in each segment.

constraints.name: sqrtx[i,
constraints.intent: The approximate square root of a route's volume is read off the piecewise-linear curve. For each route, the approximated square root equals the sum over all segments of the curve height at the segment's starting breakpoint counted when that segment is active, plus the segment slope multiplied by the fill placed in that segment.

constraints.name: sqrtp[s,
constraints.intent: The fill placed in a segment cannot exceed the room that segment offers, and a segment can only be filled when it is the active one. For each route and each genuine bounded segment, the fill amount must not exceed the segment's width, and it is forced to zero unless that segment is activated. The unbounded anchor and tail segments are not restricted this way.

constraints.name: y_seg[i,
constraints.intent: Each route may rest on at most one segment of the curve. For every route, the total number of activated segments must be at most one.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost ties together the per-route costs and the approximated square-root volumes. Set the total cost equal to the sum over all routes of the route's per-case transport cost multiplied by the approximated square root of its shipped volume.

query: We're weighing a plan to lean harder on the Seattle plant — push its combined outbound volume across all three markets up to at least 350 cases. Plug that in and tell me where the total shipping bill lands.
expected_pyomo: model.sea_total_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['seattle', j] for j in model.j) >= 350)

query: There's a proposal on the table to even out the Chicago order between the two plants — Seattle's haul into Chicago shouldn't run above half of the combined Seattle-plus-San-Diego volume going into Chicago. Plug that in and walk me through the new total cost.

query: Engineering is pitching a cap on the San Diego-to-Topeka lane so it never sits in any of its three largest size brackets — none of the top segment selectors on that lane should be switched on. Run it and tell me what it does to the total cost.

query: There's a proposal to spread San Diego's output more evenly — no single San Diego lane should carry more than 250 cases, applied to every market it serves. Run that and tell me the resulting total cost.

query: We're looking at a two-part scenario: route at least 100 cases of New York's demand through Seattle, and at the same time hold the San Diego-to-Topeka lane down to no more than 200 cases. Add both together and tell me what the total shipping cost becomes.

query: Topeka leans entirely on San Diego while Seattle never touches that market. Shouldn't Seattle at least be carrying as much of Topeka's load as San Diego does, rather than sitting it out? What's the model seeing that keeps Seattle off that lane?

model_narrative: We ship a single commodity from a set of supply plants to a set of demand markets over a fixed set of allowable routes. For each route we decide how many cases to ship. The cost of a route grows with the square root of the volume shipped on it, a concave shipping economy that is approximated by a piecewise-linear curve built from continuous per-segment fill amounts and binary per-segment activation choices. The objective is to minimize the total transportation cost across all routes.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the supply plants that ship goods

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the demand markets that receive goods

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the segments of the piecewise-linear curve that approximates the square-root cost, listed from the lowest volume range to the highest; the first segment s0 is the unbounded anchor segment below the first breakpoint and the last segment s6 is the unbounded tail segment above the last breakpoint

sets.name: sl
sets.doc: the four data fields stored for each piecewise-linear segment: x is the segment's starting volume breakpoint, y is the square-root curve value at that breakpoint, l is the horizontal length (width) of the segment, and g is the slope of the curve over the segment

sets.name: ij
sets.doc: the set of valid plant-to-market shipping routes, one ordered pair per allowable route; here every plant can ship to every market so it is the full product of plants and markets

params.name: a
params.doc: the supply capacity available at each plant, in cases; total shipments out of a plant may not exceed this

params.name: b
params.doc: the demand requirement at each market, in cases; total shipments into a market must meet at least this

params.name: d
params.doc: the distance from a plant to a market, in thousands of miles

params.name: f
params.doc: the freight rate, in dollars per case per thousand miles; a single scalar

params.name: c
params.doc: the per-case transport cost on a route, in thousands of dollars per case, computed as the freight rate times the route distance divided by one thousand

params.name: sqrtp
params.doc: the piecewise-linear curve data table indexed by segment and field; sqrtp[s,'x'] is the starting volume breakpoint of segment s, sqrtp[s,'y'] is the curve height at that breakpoint, sqrtp[s,'l'] is the width of segment s, and sqrtp[s,'g'] is the slope over segment s. The anchor segment s0 carries a sentinel width of -1 and the tail segment s6 a sentinel width of 1; only segments with a width strictly between 0 and 1000 are genuine bounded segments

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity shipped on each route, in cases

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all routes, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: delta
vars.doc: the amount of a route's shipped volume that falls within a given segment of the piecewise-linear curve, in cases; the fill of segment s for that route measured from the segment's starting breakpoint

vars.name: y_seg
vars.doc: the segment-activation indicator for a route; equals 1 if that segment of the piecewise-linear curve is the active one for the route and 0 otherwise

vars.name: sqrtx
vars.doc: the piecewise-linear approximation of the square root of the route's shipped volume

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each plant can ship out only as much as it has available. For every plant, the total shipped to all markets it serves must not exceed that plant's supply capacity.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every market's requirement must be covered. For each market, the total received from all plants that serve it must be at least that market's demand.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: The volume shipped on a route is reconstructed from the piecewise-linear segment encoding. For each route, the shipped volume equals the sum over all segments of the segment's starting breakpoint counted when that segment is the active one, plus the fill amount placed in each segment.

constraints.name: sqrtx[i,
constraints.intent: The approximate square root of a route's volume is read off the piecewise-linear curve. For each route, the approximated square root equals the sum over all segments of the curve height at the segment's starting breakpoint counted when that segment is active, plus the segment slope multiplied by the fill placed in that segment.

constraints.name: sqrtp[s,
constraints.intent: The fill placed in a segment cannot exceed the room that segment offers, and a segment can only be filled when it is the active one. For each route and each genuine bounded segment, the fill amount must not exceed the segment's width, and it is forced to zero unless that segment is activated. The unbounded anchor and tail segments are not restricted this way.

constraints.name: y_seg[i,
constraints.intent: Each route may rest on at most one segment of the curve. For every route, the total number of activated segments must be at most one.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost ties together the per-route costs and the approximated square-root volumes. Set the total cost equal to the sum over all routes of the route's per-case transport cost multiplied by the approximated square root of its shipped volume.

query: Seattle barely touches New York and runs a thin Chicago load, while San Diego shoulders the long hauls. Shouldn't Seattle's two eastern lanes — New York and Chicago together — be moving at least 350 cases between them? What's the trade-off pushing all that volume onto San Diego?

query: Seattle is hauling more than 300 cases into Chicago all on its own while other lanes sit light. Shouldn't no single Seattle lane be allowed to run above 200 cases, across every market it serves? What's keeping the optimizer from spreading Seattle's load out that way?
